So I've got kind of a, it's a pretty pedestrian question. It has to do with copy length. There's all these different kinds of things that I get in my mailbox, bank logs, and pamphlets and sales letters, and that pretty darn good outline you gave us yesterday is great, Cuddin. I'm just wondering, A, what kind of word count are there? I mean, if somebody, I would do a spec assignment or I would actually get an assignment, I wouldn't know how long, you know, I might take a sample out and count the words, but are there like page counts or word counts for that? I'll give you my answer. I'm sure other people on the panel here will have their answers too, but my rule is that you write until you run out of benefits. So if you look at the copy and there are long dead spaces where you're just trying to fill time, it's too long. If you go through it and you had to leave out important reasons why the prospect should buy, it's too short. So having said that, we are time limited when we do video scripts especially. There's going to be a target, but usually in terms of time, but usually the client will tell you, well, these are the only things that you really need to cover, you know, during this five minutes or 10 minutes or what it is. But if it's a sales page where there's no limit, then basically your job is sell product, you need to say everything that needs to be said to do that. Just to help you a little bit on the practical side, I mean, when I write a magologue, generally speaking, oh, you hear me? Do I need a microphone? Oh, sorry. I was having a sleepover. Oh, I don't need a request for it. Okay, sorry. So just to give you an idea on page count, because I agree 100% with Clayton, you write until you have made the sale. But I find that when I'm writing a magologue, generally speaking, I use, I love Courier New, I just like Courier New as a font. But if I'm using that 12, 12 point, for the most part, I'm delivering anywhere from about 38 to 46 pages of copy. And that will give you about a 24 page or 32 page magologue. If that helps. 24 for me was 32 pages, single space, Times New Rome. You know, I think the other thing too, the kind of a dimension of what Clayton was saying, is what do you want the prospect to do? I mean, if you want the prospect to just give their name, if it's kind of a lead generation, then a lot of people say too much. If you want the people to spend $2,000 for a high end investment service, then you probably can't say too much. You have to say a lot. So you really have to kind of be sensitive to what does it really take the person to convince the person of the action that I want them to take? I agree with everything everybody said to just put another dimension on it. That's one of David's favorite words to mention. Dimensionalizing that. No problem. So, you know, they said 32 pages, 34 pages, 10,000 words. If you want to do it in a word count in Microsoft Word, that'll give you like a magologue. As far as, you know, say what you need to say, right? It's often hard to know when you're writing copy because you're too close to it, right? So the rule that I use is I go through everything in that piece and I ask myself, does this help my case? Does it hurt my case? Or is it neutral? Give every sentence, every statement that litmus test. Well, if it hurts, if it helps your case, obviously you're going to keep it in there. If it hurts your case, obviously you're going to take it out. But here's the other thing. If it's neutral, take it out. Question has to do with marketing your own products. In Paris's talk, he talked about marketing the ad machine. There seems to be a lot of successful stories about freelancers, but are there successful stories about people becoming copywriters to market their own products? Yeah, there's a ton of them. You know, things are a lot easier today. Today we have the internet. You can market for free. And if you're marketing information, you can make it downloadable. Back when I did it, you know, you would have to physically print them and keep them in your garage. We didn't have VAs in the Philippines working for a dollar an hour. I mean, you had to pay people. So there's a ton of success stories out there. Look at the truth about apps. You mentioned apps. Mike Geary, he started from scratch out of his kitchen table. And he's making a gazillion dollars. And there's a lot of those guys out there. But at the core of their copywriters? Well, you have to know copy enough to do it. Now, you don't, when you're writing for your own products, you don't have to be as good as if you're writing for like a GORA or a boardroom or something. You just have to be good enough. See, at GORA and boardroom, they got a whole ton of overhead and salaries and everything else. And then they got to pay the copywriter his fee and his royalty. When it's you by your lonesome at your kitchen table, you don't have any of that stuff. And then Gary Benzavanga is not going to write a control and beat you. You don't have to worry about that either. Right, exactly. There's a guy that started out as a copywriter and he went into his own business. And last I heard he was deciding whether or not to get a G5 private jet in his Porter Stansbury. Bill Bonner owns all of the GORA and all the affiliated businesses started out as a copywriter. A friend of mine, Joe Sugarman, started out life as a copywriter. And now he owns, he's got blue blocker sunglasses. He's got the 3D night vision goggles that you see on television, all that kind of stuff. So Ted Nicholas is another one. The copywriter entrepreneur is throughout America and throughout the history of direct response marketing. Jay Peterman, you just go on and on and on. The guy that started Moncomber Reward was not a trained copywriter. He was not a salesman at the time, but he started that store by writing flyers and sending them out to people. So there's an awful lot of precedent for that. And when I urge people to start their own companies on the side, I urge copywriters, start your own company on the side. I don't care if you're selling urschus or it doesn't matter, right? What you're doing by doing that is you now have a stake in the success of that business. And you're learning a lot of the other things that you need to learn in order to make it a success. Paper click, SEO, all of those kinds of things. It's worth it just for that, for how closely you're going to follow the results. Because you never follow things so closely as when it's your own. It's like Clayton said, investing in the stock market. It's the exact same thing. You do your own product, you get to feel the results, you get to understand direct mail in a way that you just don't when you're working for a client. And I had a second question. Oh, unless there's one. As for Lori, I was telling Lori I have a daughter in art school and there's a couple programs for graphic design. But is there kind of a defined path outside of going to school and getting those programs? Is there a good way to get involved in that on the graphic design end of it? I would just say to that perhaps have her learn some of the software. And again, like Katie said, we have so many different programs available, the graphic design for success, and different packages that can also tie in the copywriting and the graphic design. That I think just getting out there and trying some of these packages and then getting a plan of attack. Tierra kind of started that way, right? You started with one type of software and then started on little projects and work your way up. But I can talk to you as well, you know, after the event and give you a better plan of attack. I would love to help you, but we have so many things here that we can help her with definitely. There was a question here. Thank you all so much. This has been life changing. So, kind of a question just about how to run the business. Virtual assistants, thank you for bringing that up. When would you hire them? At what point and what would you have them doing exactly so that you can streamline your time to be work better faster? Whenever you figure out that you're spending more time doing stuff that doesn't make you money. You'll know what those tasks are. I don't, you know, maybe it's paying the bills, maybe it's answering the phone, maybe it's organizing your schedule, maybe it's some other, you know, maybe it's research, maybe it's some other function. But, you know, just keep that principle in mind, though. The principle that applies to research, you only make maximum money when you're writing copy. You don't make maximum money when you're researching or doing all of those collateral duties. So, the more of that you can offload, you know, given a viable client. Let's say, you know, you have to have a viable client before you start doing all of this, but the more of that stuff you can offload, the more time you can spend on that client's work, the bigger successes you can write, and the more quantity you can put out. And you'll know, you know, you won't have to have a plan for when you're going to offload this stuff. It'll be really obvious to you. You'll be sitting there, you know, balancing your checkbook or something. Go, my God, I'm on deadline. I need to get back to this work, you know, so. And just, you know, an example of how you'll know, you'll know. I mean, I'll tell you my example. I know I needed to get some extra help, and it was when I had to research on a project, and it was about, you know, a gout. So I started going, at first I started looking up on arthritis, and I went into gout, and I went gout, okay, well that happens to your feet, feet, okay, that means shoes, shoes. Oh, my God, this is a sale. And I ended up ordering five pairs of shoes online. And that's when I went, okay, this is not good. This is not helping me. So the things you don't like to do is what you want to get rid of. What you want to get rid of. If you're not writing copy, you're not making money. And I had a wonderful assistant for three years, and she moved away, and I haven't gotten another one yet, but it was just wonderful because she took care of everything I didn't want to go. She was my wife. You get that? We talked about that in the conversation. No, it wasn't Roz. This was Shannon. Roz is on her own doing her own thing. And I said, oh, great, this is what it must mean to have a wife, you know? Because it was like, I didn't want to go to a grocery store. She took care of it. Just everything that, because I'd use those as excuses not to write. I'm like, oh, I got to take care of my kids. I got to do that. No, I don't. They're fine. You know? A little pain never hurt anybody. So that's when you'll know. You have to look at yourself and be honest and say, what is stopping me from writing copy? And then you'll find what you need. Do the math. How much do you get paid for an assignment? How many hours does it take you to do it? Whatever that number is, let's say just for, you know, round number, $100 an hour. If you're cleaning your own house, that means you're a $100 an hour housekeeper. So it's a very simple decision. As soon as possible, offload as much as you can. Now, if you enjoy it, if it's your hobby, then do it yourself. But if not, then get the hell off your plane. I haven't mowed a blade of grass in 30 years. That was one of the first things I identified, right? I figured exactly what Paris said. I'm getting paid. You know, it's costing me $100 an hour to mow this grass. What about the weed? Yeah, the same thing. The weed. Same thing, I guess. No, no, no. That's the weed. Oh, no, no. We, we, I hate, I hate left brain stuff like balancing checkbooks and paying bills. When Wendy and I got together, we found out that she hates doing, she hates watching me spend money. So it caused a lot of strife in the family when she was balancing the checkbook. So we hired a girl 20 years, 18. Like 16, 17 years ago, Tanya. And Tanya has handled our money every day since then. She doesn't get to see what I spend unless she asks about it. Yes, I do. Yes, she does. But at least we don't have the fights over it anymore. And, and, you know, we don't have to do that. We don't have to do that, that kind of time-wasting thing for us. Anybody else? Hi. Any other questions? How much say so do the copywriters get in the design and layout of their copy? None. See, now we're coming. You want to take this? You may want to take this? Oh, okay. Okay. When you turn in your final draft and you, you submit your payment for your, you know, for the balance of your, your fees, you are not done. Usually in the process, I try to set up a time to have a conversation with the, with the designer. The designer is going to make or break your package. You know, you can spend all this time, you know, writing it, but if you don't have it designed right, you're screwed, you know. And so the designer is a very important person in the whole process. So I, I, I like to choose my designer. If I tell them initially, hey, I want to work with this person for this project. And if I don't have that choice, I want to know who I'm working with. I have a conference call with the designer to give them ideas of what I'm thinking about it. In the copy, I have notes for the designer throughout. Like I'm thinking about this here. I'm thinking about this here. So it's very important. I don't want the designer to show the client the layout until I'm happy with it. You know, because if she or he shows it to the client, to the client, the client's happy with it and I'm not, it's going to get changed again. So it's like, I want to feel like when we present it as a team, this is, you know, and usually a smart client will say, well, has the copywriter approved this, you know, to ask the designer a question. So when the designer is giving the, the, the design to the, the client, it is final almost for me. And now the client has a chance to give any type of feedback he or she has on it. And then we'll go ahead and finalize at that point. So the answer is that the copywriter should have a lot of say so, but at the same time, the copywriter should not be trying to design the package for the designer because you're hiring an expert to do this. So you have to have a confidence level on the person who's designing it to let them do their jobs. You know, I am not a designer. I'll do stick figures to go, you know, happy person, you know, or something. This is what I want here. But I want someone who understands direct response, who, who I, who I work well with and we get each other and then let them do their thing. And it's a beautiful thing when it happens that way. Okay. Okay. You got to think about what is the purpose of design? What's the difference between good design and bad design? What does design bring to the success or failure, the promotion? If you have a designer that doesn't even read the copy, you know, and just starts laying out masses of black ink and so forth. You got a bad designer. You got to fire that person. Lori said this yesterday. She reads a copy carefully. She gets passionate about the product. She gets passionate about the copy. Okay. The designer that your job is to write a persuasive argument. The designer's job is to make sure it gets seen in red. Right. And there are certain rules and Lori could probably do a whole weekend on this, but there are certain rules about readership. What draws the eye? What causes people to stop and to read something? What can a designer do to make a good headline work even better? You know, what kind of images should you use in order to draw the eye? How do women respond differently than men to certain images? You know, a good designer will know all of that. So it's more than knowing how to run in design or some other, you know, the page layout software. It's got to be a partner in building big response. That's what you're looking for. Oh, and I have a rule with my clients and it's the same rule Carling just said. Nobody sees the design until I say it's ready. So there was a study done. Let me see here. There was a study done. I think it was Macmillan that did it. You can look up readership studies and this readership study was in over a period of 10 or 12 years. And it really wasn't about copy. It was about what design elements increase readership of an ad. And they found out things, for example, like a storyboard type of ad is the most effective at creating high readership levels. They found out that men prefer pictures of modes of transportation that that will draw their eye quickest to an ad, whereas women prefer pictures of other women. Okay. The lowest on the level of images that attract men were children. The lowest on the woman's list of images was men. But the studies on readership... Do you think there's any population growth at all? Yeah. No. So there's a lot that goes into line length and type size and always think about your market too because you've got, I mean, I've market older people. If some designer comes in and they do this all the time, it should be crazy, especially online. And they'll come in with type that is not only 9.8 point type that you can't possibly read. It's not even black. It's gray on white. It's like they're doing everything they can possibly think of to make sure nobody reads this. And so that's why it's so important to have a Lori Heller or Rob Davis or Ted Kecoller who understand the direct response ramifications of design. There. You know, one thing about type just reminded me of the kind of stuff you should know. Like Bill Royal tells a story about how he decided maybe we should try testing this with the type a little bit bigger and it raised response. They try to get maybe we should just type a little bit bigger since we got a little more response, a little more and a little more. He said, it just got so big I couldn't make the type any bigger anymore. But it kept raising response. So, you know, that's the power of design, you know? Yeah. Great. There's actually a couple articles on our total package website. One's called Graphics 101. And then there's another one about punctuation that is a pet peeve of glaze. Placer fees. Yeah. Just further to the design questions. Do you ever put it in your contract one like about having a designer or seeing it before it goes to production? Or is that just something that is assumed? Maybe. And also too, what about the difference between seeing online offline? You know, it's one thing like Carleen, you were describing how you see it before it goes and what about when they are doing it online? Online is a mixed bag. It's very difficult because, you know, you press what is it? Caps lock and shift and then plus, plus, plus, plus, and the page, the web page gets bigger. You know, or minus, minus, minus, and it gets smaller. And everybody's monitor is set up differently and everybody's browser interprets the HTML code differently. So, it's very hard online to judge, to get too specific with some design elements. For example, it's very important on a sales page to have the beginning of the copy and the first few paragraphs above the fold. Right? So, when you come to the sales page, you get your headline, your deck, and your first few paragraphs, a copy, are immediately visible. Right? But how can you determine that with a variety of zoom ins and zoom outs and browsers and so forth? So, that's a difficult one. What was the other part of your question? Contracts. Contracts. No, I don't. Does anybody here put that in icon? Usually that's a conversation with a client. Yeah. And usually a verbal understanding. Yeah. And they want to make you happy. They want you to write a winner and come back and write another one. So, it's usually not a big deal. You mentioned that there one trend in copies is more of less high B, more logical type copy. What are the trends do you see as far as copy and marketing that you're seeing with your clients and in the market in general? You mean beyond that? Okay. Anybody? Okay. I think the most important thing and one of the most important things, especially coming from studying product launches and the whole product launch thing that's happening now in the industries, and all the industries is chunking the copy and not necessarily the serializing like Clayton was discussing earlier. Not 80 page sales letters or whatever, but little at a time because everybody's attention span is getting so much smaller from all the social media, all the electronics, especially our kids. The kids attention span these days is just ridiculous. So, you know, you're starting to see that feed in where you have to present small chunks of copy and get their attention that way rather than trying to just overwhelm them because people get overwhelmed really fast. You have heard of video sales letters, right? Oh, yeah. Okay. So obviously that's been the big one for over the past five years. And in a way it's what Paul was just talking about. It's chunking them screen by screen like a PowerPoint. So, you know, that's a big one. And now they have, it's morphed into interview style videos. Have you seen those two talking heads so that, you know, things come and go, right? Infomercials were big in the 50s. They disappeared because she got outlawed and Reagan came back and said, what the hell is this illegal for? Then they became big again. Then it became too expensive to do them. So, you know, it's just like wide ties, skinny ties, short hams, long hams, things come in and out of fashion. But the fundamentals never change because human nature never changes. Yeah, people have shorter attention spans. But the first VSL I did, I didn't know any better. So I didn't put the buy button till an hour and 20 minutes into it. And people sat through the whole damn thing because the copy, you want to have copy that's gripping that they can't stop watching. It keeps intriguing them, surprising them, shocking them, putting little unanswered questions in front of them. Joe Sugarman calls it seeds of curiosity. Just keep people reading. And for the most part, we're still talking to a 50 plus year old market. You know, young people definitely are having shorter attention span because of, you know, the social media and whatnot. But we're still predominantly talking to readers, you know, older readers who are accustomed to, you know, longer copies. So it's not, to me, it's not long copy, short copy. It's compelling copy versus boring copy because you can bore the heck out of me with short copy. You've lost my attention, you know, but like Paris was saying, if it's compelling and you've got me nodding my head, I'm going to keep reading it. I don't have a point where I say, oh, it's been five pages. I better stop. I stop when I'm not interested anymore. So your job is to keep me interested until you get me to click that button to order. You know, I think I covered our experience with this and that is when we map, you know, you know what heat maps are? I didn't. I didn't until yesterday. Oh, really? Okay. Well, it's pretty cool. You know, you slap a sales page up on the Internet and there's this technology that can tell you where the eye goes, where they lose interest. You know, if there's a lot of interest in an area, it's shaded red, okay, bright red or orange, and then all the way down to blue or even white, you know, it's an area that people didn't even look at. So you have those kind of heat maps on the sales page. You can, if you're writing for the Internet, you ask your prospect, your client, to please heat map this page. So after it appears the first time, you go into the page and you look at all of the areas where you were losing readership and you strengthen them, you pump them up, okay? As far as videos concerned, Google Analytics will tell you, I drew the graph for you the other day, remember, at the beginning of the video, here's your viewership and then you'll see it just drop about halfway down to zero at about two minutes and then it'll stay pretty steady until about five minutes and then it drops back down again. So there are some time limitations on video that you need to be aware of, just be aware of. It doesn't mean you can't do an hour and 20 minute BSL, but the trend seems to be among most people I've been talking to and certainly with us that we're serializing our video sales letters rather than presenting them all at once. There's a website and I can't remember the name of it, but I could probably dig it up on my computer, that does what you were talking about on a much more granular level. So you could see the little tiny variations and I have a buddy who's made a fortune just by doing that. He looks at the videos, he finds where they're lagging, what was being said at that moment and then he changes it and goes right back up, responds. He's been able to get huge bumps in response by doing nothing other than, you don't have to be a genius copywriter, you just look at what the data is telling you. And I'll try to find the name of the website. It is expensive though. You ready, Alice? I have a question about cliches and headlines. I'm working on a weight loss product and I was just looking at all the magazines, women's magazines for April and every single one of them said lose seven pounds in seven days, 21 pounds in 21 days. And I thought, well, if everybody's using it, is it a good thing because it really sells or is it like overdone? Is that a cliche now? Let's take that one. Those things are there because they work, so then you have to assume they work. But how can you say it in a way that's interesting? How can you say it in a way that's powerful? How can you say it in a way that's different? How can you make the statistics different from that? You know, lose half a pound in a minute. You know, lose 20 pounds in a month. How do you turn it into a testimonial? How do you add all those things Clayton said? The drama, the sex appeal, the irony. How do you make it ironic how I lost 20 pounds with kinfoil? What? I mean, that's curiosity. Some little mechanism to it. So, you know, when you're looking at those magazines, you know, look at the ones that don't do it. Look at the ones that really do that well, like Woman's World or someplace like that, that really do make the uniqueness of it. Or look at how they do it with something that's not weight loss and how you could apply that to weight loss. Like maybe Cosmo is doing it with sex really well. So how do you transpose that, like we do in music, into something with weight loss? Great. Thank you. He's right. They're doing it because it works. But as you pointed out, there is a point at which it doesn't work anymore. And how do you know what that point is? Well, on a most basic level is if you're starting to get tired of it because you've seen it a million times, then maybe it's not working. But realize that you're more aware than the prospect is because you're looking at it specifically for them. It's just background stuff. So, you know, one rule of thumb I use to see if something's new to people is if I've read it seven different times and seven different places, then probably by now people are catching up. It seems that that seven seems to be like a magic number. Another thing is if... You track that in a list somewhere that you have in your... What's it six? What's it five? So, just kind of, you know, like, you get a set. But here's another thing is if you can ever get an opportunity to write for Rodale or write for Cosmo or write for one of these big ass magazine publishers, they will tell you what their new stand sales were. That was like the greatest thing. When I got hired to write for Prevention Magazine and Men's Health Magazine, they gave me the last two years, here's the image of that cover and what the new stand sales. And you start to see the patterns, what gets people to buy... That was fascinating to me. Give the mic. It was fascinating to me when I was writing for Rodale. And they gave me the new stand sales for different headlines and covers for Prevention and Men's Health. Nowadays, you pick up a magazine at the store and you'll see a little line under that says one of two covers. And so they're getting smart. You know, they're starting to test one cover against each other. So now they're not just stumbling on ideas, they're actually developing them and testing them. So I see them evolving even faster in the years ahead. So it's really a cool way to go. Also, a lot of these magazines are now read online on iPads and so forth. And so that gives them the data instantly as to which magazine covers are actually being read. So, Clay. Some of you may be able to validate this too, but I know one thing I'll use is... You know how you all have the homepage like on Expedia or like my... I use Roadrunner and Cincinnati. So for my internet access... Sorry, I'm really tired today. So when I log on to check my email remotely, not using my Outlook account, okay? It's feeding up news stories and ads to me, okay? Those are all measured. So one thing you can do is look to see what's coming through all those portals, right? And how are they phrasing that? Because in terms... I used to use it to get subject line ideas all the time because they have to be short, right? Because they're the same thing that Clay and I talk about what fits on a screen, right? And they know I'm just looking at my... Logging into my email account, but they're trying to catch me with news about something else that's going on. And there's tons of weight loss stuff there. I mean, we all... When you were talking about... I can't remember what you said earlier. Weird. All the women on the side of the room, you didn't hear us. We all went, the one weird flat belly... Yeah, yeah. See, because we all knew that ad because we've seen it many times. Oh, the one that... You know, there's another one about increasing the size of a guy's junk, you know? I mean, it's a one weird trick that does this one way. And people just love that stuff. And it's been working for years now on lines. So that's one tip on the financial side. And I'm assuming this is still true. And I don't think I'm giving away any trade secrets. If you sign up for the Motley Fool Watch Weekly, the Motley Fool measures everything. I mean, they probably know, you know, how many kernels of popcorn the ordinary American eats every night at the movie theater. I don't know. They measure everything. And when you get Fool Watch Weekly, that is the highest rated, opened, read stories. Okay. From all of the stuff they're publishing everywhere, the free access stuff. So when you get Fool Watch Weekly, and it gives you five links and they're in order and they have the headline of the story, you know, from that massive audience, those were the ones that people were clicking on and reading. Now, that's not talking about converting that to sales or anything, but you're knowing what's a conversation that's going on about finance and their audience right now. So if you look over several weeks and you see that there seems to be the word dividend coming through all the time on Fool Watch Weekly, well, then that's a big conversation with investors right now. So I think you can use these things online as well as the old traditional stuff of looking at the bullets and the magazines. And there's lots of little ways to notice that. And then add all these twists these guys are telling you. Great, great, great. Does anybody, well, so they all have weekly easing. Will Mark call us the big guy. I'm sorry. I was just going to say, there's a way to short circuit that process. You can go to the source that Motley Fool used to determine those rankings, which is Google Analytics. And so you go on Google Analytics and you can find out what search terms are trending. And so, you know, yeah, on there. So, but this gives you phrases that you can then twist and use for that gives you the this will give you the that if you go to the Fool Watch Weekly, you'll see the whole phrase is what I used to dummy up for ideas for things. And then you're right. Mercola will tell you how many people click I use that all the time to look up stuff on tell about Mercola. Well, I don't know a whole lot about Mercola, but I wanted to clarify something I said before when I said seven times. Seven times doesn't mean it's played out seven times means it's finally on people's radar. And then after that, if you're seeing it a gazillion times after that, now you know, like everybody here's the other thing I wanted to say is, you know, I mentioned Rodale before Cosmo, the longer you're in this business and the longer you get more and more clients, they tell you their trade secrets because they want you to get a winner. Now, you're not allowed to divulge them. You may have to sign a non disclosure agreement and so forth, which is fine because I never tell anybody anything. I don't tell client a what's going on with client B. You just don't do that. But I can't help but be influenced by what they told me. I can't unlearn what they told me and it influences everything I do. And when you start getting all this intelligence from all these different sources, you just have this bigger global understanding of what's going on. And it's like the book blink. You guys have read the book blink. It's just, you know instantly you see something and you know whether whether, you know, you don't know how you know, but you just do another thing. I just want to say unrelated, but I wanted to comment on what you said, Marcella, about the short subject lines. We're finding recently a lot of evidence that the longer subject lines work better. And I have a theory that if the subject lines too long for them to read it in their preview on their web web web outlook or whatever it is, they don't know what the hell is. So now they have to click on it to read it. There was an interesting, speaking of subject lines, there was an interesting thing that was told us at the round table and that was the guys were finding their best subject lines were subject lines that identified the reader as somebody who had heard from this writer before. So you know, since you did this yesterday, why haven't I heard from you? Something like that. You know, you asked for this here, you know, here it is. Anything that sends the message that that this is this is from a friend. This is from somebody that yeah, there's a relationship here. I got one. I got one yesterday and the subject line was FW colon with nothing next to it. So you assume they're forwarding you something right the FW colon. Right or R E colon, but those only work so long before they catch on that you have to use them randomly. The other thing that we're seeing that works sometimes with subject line is alternating between one that's sort of like clearly telegraphing the benefit of reading this like this is going to expire at midnight tonight, your last chance to see whatever. And one that's just like a random phrase that a friend would put up there, you know, about dinner tonight or, you know, and you can just connect it somehow in the opening of your copy. But if it seems like an email that is not from a salesperson, but from a friend, we're also overwhelmed. We're so busy. We're just scrolling through the box. I'll sometimes they catch me and I crack up and I have on my outlook. I have just like, I think we've talked as much about swipe files here, but I have my outlook subject line swipe file and anytime something grabs me and I open it and they got me. And I'm like, oh my God, they got me to open that and I thought it was something, you know, in it goes in it goes so have your subject line swipe files to. I think Kelvin's next. Yeah, I have a question for Guillermo. And I really loved your presentation. And, you know, you address something that's universal and it's usually the number one and an initial reason why most people don't succeed is because of their fear and they just don't get going. So my question for you is, when are you going to release your book on this subject? Oh, of course. Guillermo, I've written a title for you. You can just consider this if you like it. It's how to turn your fears into a fortune. Yeah. Thank you. Unless of course you're afraid to do that. Soon. Soon. So where to answer this, Guillermo? Every month or so for the last three years, I've been getting a one page solicitation from State Farm that says you too can save, you know, 40% if you combine your house and your car and blah, blah, blah, blah, blah. And it's the most boring thing and I, you know, it hasn't changed in forever. And I called one of the agents and said, you know, what do you think about this? And she says, I hate it because people come to me and say, well, you can, you know, and then she has to tell them, no, really, I can't save you 40%. Oh, that's nice. So they don't like it, but they said it's sent from corporate and we don't have any control over it. If I, I mean, and I look at this each time it comes in before I throw it out and I think, man, you know, this only touches people who are only driven by finance. We had a tornado go through. I feel so much more, it would resonate with me if they said your State Farm will be there when the tornado comes in a picture of them, you know, comforting. And I know there are compliance issues, but is there, do you have any suggestions on if I wanted to say, hey guys, I think I'd like to try to do a little better for you. Don't don't fight corporations. Yeah, it's not worth it. There's so many businesses out there that need copy that you can work with the owner. You can work with the decision maker to try to try to try to get State Farm to change their ways. Yeah, you know, remember, I said the other day that one of the qualifications of a great client is that they understand the primacy of copy. Right. State Farm doesn't. And that's why, you know, they're paying some poor schmuck, you know, 50 grand a year to write that stuff. And they never test anything against it. And, you know, they don't understand the primacy of copy. You know, if they ever did a single test, you know, if they ever gave you a shot at it, you'd beat the pants off of that thing, you know, but they just don't do it. It's too deeply ingrained in them corporately. A smaller company like State Farm, maybe in the same business, you might have a chance, you know, but that's the beauty of capitalism, I think. The old companies get all stodgy and boring and slow and bureaucratic and some new upstart comes along and just blows them away. Yeah, with some lizard, right? Right. Or flow. You know, flow. You got anything? Nope. Okay. One more question. You mentioned the headline about the, by this magazine or I'll shoot the dog. Were you using that as an example of a good headline that worked? My follow-up question that is, when I think about a headline, I think about something that someone once told me that the headline's job is to get the person to read the rest, but it's also to get them to read it in the right mood. So I was wondering about that. Yeah. Okay. No, I was, I was, I was talking really about the stopping power of a headline. You know, it's like in guns, a nine millimeter doesn't have as much stopping power as a 45, right? So there's relative stopping power. That's the function of the headline is to stop me in my tracks and make me read the deck, right? So, I mean, your headline could be sex. Now that I have your attention, you know, I mean, whatever is going to stop the guy. I'm not seriously recommending that that you have a headline that's completely unrelated to the content of your message. But I was just trying to make the point that the purpose of the headline is to stop you in your tracks. You know, and sometimes things like that, it's not so much you want to do that headline or headline like that, but how can you infuse your headline with that kind of stopping power? You know, it's like Jim Rutz did a headline for a health promotion, read this or die. It's the same sort of thing, but much more appropriate. Right. He probably, I'm sure he stole that idea from National Lampoon. No, read this or die. There was an old ad, one of the old ad agencies like Lord and Thomas or something, they had a headline read this or die and Rutz just swiped it. Wow, I didn't know that. That was one of the all time great headlines in the health field. Oh, nothing. Any more questions? Well, you are now in your career, especially as you're starting out. It's a very good idea to learn about headlines, the various types of headlines. So, you know, spend the time on, you know, understanding a fascination headline or a testimonial headline, just understanding that, that there are certain rules that go along with it. And now when you learn the rules, break them. You know, I do it all the time. I mean, if I was being critiqued on my headline, you know, for the seven points that go along to making a successful headline, I probably would fail 50% of the time. I had a headline and with a package that ran for about two years and the headline was, who to thunk it? That's all. I mean, you would never know is for a liver and kidney cleanse. You know, you would never have known that, you know, but to me, the purpose of the headline is to grab your attention. That's all I want them to stop for a second and see what it is. I don't want the headline. It's not setting a mood for me. It's not going to make the sale for me. It's just going to stop them right there so I can now I've got your attention and then get them into the copy. So keep it simple in essence. Like I said, learn the rules and feel free to break them, you know, when you need to, because you can get some, you used to tease me all the time. Because one year for some reason, all my winning headlines, oh no, Wendy teased me about it. All my headlines for that year, that one started with the word got. Got gas. Got joint pain. I mean, it was, if it had got in it and I filled in the blank, I had a control. I don't know. So, you know, did I mess with that? Oh heck no. Every time I had a chance, I put a test of the headline with the word got on it and it worked. But that doesn't necessarily fit a, you know, a typical, you know, description of what a headline is supposed to do and all that kind of stuff. So learn the rules and feel free to break them. I think there's just this is kind of one of my pet peeves. I think there's, and I got a lot of them, but there's, I think there's a lot of nonsense that passes for copywriting training. There was one guy that was all about hypnotic copywriting. How you're going to hypnotize your prospect. Yeah, I'm not naming names. Just when? All kinds of names. Yeah, I guess I did. But in a good way, right? Martin? Oh, that's right. God, I hope he never hears this. Anyway, so yeah, there's a lot of stuff where people are reaching for something that sounds exotic or interesting or really different or, you know, like the big secret and so forth. But when was the last time you read a headline and your mood changed? You know, do you really think that happens? Hemingway. No. You know that story? No. Okay. Let me get this right so I don't screw it up. Somebody made a bet with Hemingway that he couldn't move them in like a short, six words. Do you remember the exact thing? It's the shoes. Right. You're going to test me on this. He said I can make you like cry in six letters, six words or something. Tell an entire story. Tell an entire story. And I know I'm going to screw this up, but it was like, baby shoes for sale, never worn. Never worn. Oh my gosh. Wow. See, okay, see, right. I stand corrected. And there's a website where people do that that you sent us to as one of our lessons. Can you see how amazing it is to be taught by this guy, right? So we all went to that website and we're just reading them and it did spark ideas for me for how to pack the emotional content that Clayton just does intuitively. I do not. So it like was a good lesson for me to see how much you could, how much emotion can you pack into six words. So are you going to use that as a headline? No. But could you see how reading a whole bunch of them, you know, it's like writing haiku, right? I had a friend who used to write one every month. Like you start to see how you can do that because I am not naturally like Clayton. I'm more left brain. So I'm more like a how to plum your sink and six steps using a wrench. And then, you know, David's like, you know, it's also, it's not, you have to look at the totality of things. You know, it's not like the headline. It's how the headline goes into the subhead, how that goes into the lead. So you have a headline like Arthur Johnson had a thing about had enough. And it wasn't like that was such a brilliant headline, but the whole concept of how that went into, look, these days they're telling you not to, not to drink coffee, not to eat, not to eat meat, to do this, to do this. If you had enough, I'm going to, my health letter is going to tell you all about how you can do all these things and still live to be 120. So that was the brain, just like, you know, Carlene with the, what was it, can you want? Who to thank it? Yeah, who to thank it. It's how that went into the copy and led into the Colin Clem stuff and how she transitioned into that, how she made that work. Not whether that's a good headline or a bad headline in and of itself. One more thing. I was thinking also, a lot of times you'll have a client who will ask you for pieces of your work. You know, oh, let me have the headline, let me have the, you know, the debt copy, you know, let me have this so that they can kind of check you as you're going along. It can do a lot with younger writers to make sure that you're kind of on the right track. I could not do that to save my life because sometimes the headline is the absolute last thing that I come up with. Sometimes it's the absolute first thing. I'm like, this is the headline. I am writing a package with that headline, you know, or what the heck is my headline? Let me go back to this package and, you know, figure out what it is and I'll get it at the last minute. So to me, I think it kind of handicaps a writer when you have to, you know, deliver partial work to a client because the work is evolving. I mean, you know, I don't know what I'm going to write that day. I may feel like just, you know, may have a real sucky day. I just want to write boring stuff. So I'll just go ahead and go, get put a stupid bio on here, put the order from here, whatever, you know, other days is like so creative. I just want to write headlines all day long, you know, so it's all just, I mean, I'm very intuitive with my writing. I don't do outlines. I suck at outlines. I was the kid in high school in school that you had to turn an outline in before you did your term paper. I wrote the term paper and then I did that because I couldn't, it wouldn't have mattered. I wouldn't have stuck to it anyway. So it's just one of those things where it's like if someone's asking you to write, to do something that's not your style of writing, speak up and let them know. I've turned down, I turned down jobs early in my career when a client says, well, we need you have your outline, you know, for your package. I'm like, well, I can't give it to you because I don't know. And if I gave it to you, it's going to change anyway. So why are you wasting my time? Let me write you a control, you know, so just kind of start to understand who you are, the kind of person you are. Marcella knows, she says she's a left brain thinker. I'm not, you know, but you're in nothing wrong with either way. You have brilliant people who are both right and left brain thinkers. It's just know who you are and know how you like to work and be true to yourself and let the client know my job is to give you a control. How I do it is my business. You know, it's not going to, you don't have to get involved in it. Well, say it nicer. But you did say it nicer in the beginning. You said a very reasonable explanation and who's going to argue with that, right? So, you know, speak up, don't be afraid to speak up, but be nice about it. No fear. I like to make a comment on this because I've credit a lot of copy. And first of all, don't be afraid. Give your package anything you write to someone else to read it and give you their emotional response on it. The number one can get their emotional reaction to it. And quite often when I would read some of the copywriter's packages here, Paul can probably validate this. I would read it and the headline was really in the subhead or the debt copy. People struggle so much sometimes for that perfect headline that they missed the most valuable point that came kind of easier to them earlier. And so quite often I'd be like circling that going put that up there and put that down there. And that always works a lot well for people. Yeah, the same thing happens with the lead copy. When I'm creating a cub's copy nine times out of ten, the lead's on page three. And he just buried his lead. It's like one of the most cliche mistakes a copywriter can make. I'd be interested just as a rule, you know, when do you guys write your headlines as a rule? I know you mix it up, but is there one way or do you write your headlines first? Or do you write your...you tend to write them first or you tend to write them later in the process? Well, like I said, I change. It just depends. If I know...if I know...if I have a gut feeling what the headline is, I'll throw up some stuff in there or play with it a day of just doing nothing, but headline to see if something resonates or at least, you know, something that's close to what I want, and then I'll keep tweaking it. So I may find a good point that may make the headline stronger by adding this word to it or whatever. But I don't...I don't have a system of, let's get the headline done this time and to this point. But no system, but just typically which way... When I go, wow! Something I read, something I wrote or whatever, and like, you know, when something does that to me, that's a potential headline. That's when you get... But, E.G., for me it's typically the headline first, because I like to have something I can go back to and keep everything on track and keep with the whole power of one. But it's subject to change, though. If after writing everything, I sense that that original headline I went with still doesn't feel right, or it doesn't feel as good as I thought it did, then I'll change it. Lori, this is where I hit the line. No, but how well she's designing them. I actually was going to say something about the change in print format. Right now, a lot of people are doing the 8.5 by 11 MAGA logs, as you know. So then they have to fold them in half these days, or most people are. So it's a whole different ball of wax. Have you guys been experiencing this? Where in the past, it would be that front cover, the 8.5 by 11, where that hard-hitting headline is. And then maybe they would give me a back cover headline as well. Sometimes I've seen those two switch. We went out the whole way, you know, with that front headline that they wrote. But now, not only do you have that to contend with, you're folding it in half, so it's like, you know, a number 10 envelope. So all those things come into play as well. Oh, sure. Absolutely. For me, remember, I was talking about how to let the project tell you how it wants to get done. So, you know, sometimes I'll be jumping into a project, and I'll have a pretty good headline idea. But I think most of the time, my headline isn't final until I'm on my final draft. And I've had a lot of time to think about it. I've had a lot of time to sleep on it. And even if I think I have a headline, it's the last thing I look at again before I send off to the client. But nine times out of ten, it'll be, I'm writing a copy, and like Carlene said, something I write. And Bob King taught me this. He said, we have to do some headline tests. He said, instead of trying to come up with something out of whole cloth, why don't you read through the mag-a-log that you wrote and just highlight the phrases that really have high impact for you and see if there's a potential there to write a great headline for a test. So, but I tend to do it late in the process. How about you? Yeah, I mean, I totally agree. It's, you know, I'll write a headline sometimes in a subhead, start writing the copy, but the headline is totally subject to change. And a lot of times you'll go back. In retrospect, it won't make you go, wow, there'll be stuff that you've written. The copy will take a different direction. I'm very, what Jim Roth's called, lapidary. You know, it's like chiseling the copy, changing it, you know, throwing it out if it doesn't work, starting again. And it's the same way with the headlines until finally it just rings. Like Carlene said, you read it and you go, wow, and you know, that's where it is. How about you, Paris? It varies with me. I'd say probably most of the time I write the headline for us. But when I'm copy chiefing somebody almost always, I picked their headline out of page three, or page five, or in the subhead or something. Because they, when you're close to it very often, you don't see it for what it is. And it takes another set of eyes to go, oh my God, this is it. The leads right here, this is the part that jazzed me, or as Marty Edelston would say, it made me vibrate. You're a little Marty. Yeah, you said vibrate her. You had to go there. Sorry. But she didn't have the mic, so it's not what you're saying. Okay, Paul? Headline. I always start with the headline. In fact, for me, I just have this hang-up where if I don't have a headline on the paper, I can't write the rest, anything. I can't write a word until I have a headline down. But for me, the headline sets the tone of the entire package. So, you know, I like to sit and I'll sit forever, I'll sit for days if I have to, and just think about the prospect and where they're at, and what the trends in the market are, and what I want them, you know, what are they feeling at the moment that they get this package, and that they see me in the sales letter, and what phrase, what feeling do I need to create to make them stop, like we've been talking about. But, you know, nine times out of ten, the headline will change before I'm done. I mean, but I've got to have, I feel like I have to have that. I've got to have the starting place. And the rest of the package falls in. It depends on if I'm working with Paris or David. If I'm working with Paris and we're working on one of the packages that we've had for a while as a control, and we're going to do a test, we're going to start with the headline because that gives me the concept. And so what I'll do is I'll look at all the information, and I'll come up with seven or eight headlines that each one of them is focusing on a different angle or way in. Then I'll send it to him, and he'll be like, no, I don't like this one. We pick the ones that he kind of is interested in the angle on the way in, and then he says, go run the hundred. You know, go give me a hundred of those substitute different words, try different things, let's see what works. And in general, we'll solidify the headline for the test that we're going to do. And then I can write the opening that I'm going to put on the existing package to go with that. With David, if we're doing a new package, and because he never likes to close something down and has been known to pull blues and things off of printing presses to change a word in the headline, it's always a thing in motion, but we try to have the concept. And so we'll put down, we just start sending back and forth in words what we think the idea of the headline is, but we don't have the words polished yet. And then as we go along there, we're playing around with them, we're getting more. So this one that we just did for Boredrum, we kept saying reach for this first, because the idea was home cures that you would go grab something, reach for this first. And we got all excited, and we loved the words, and we thought this was really cool. And then over the last couple of days here, as we're seeing it laid out, we realized it was too abstract. We couldn't make a picture that made you understand what reach for this first meant, even though in words we thought we had it. So we ended up at the last minute changing that and tweaking that, and I can't tell you that I have to kill you. Going back, interestingly, going back to something that we had tried in the beginning, an idea that you had come up with, that I was like, no, this isn't working, no, this isn't working, we couldn't make it work, we gave up on it, and then when we reached for this first, it was like that adrenaline of holy mackerel, we got to make something work, and you managed to take that and make it work in a way that was just, you went wow. After that all came together. The thing that David and I do that I would encourage you that helps us get the headline that's really great is we argue our way into it, like an old Jewish married couple. So I call and say, I had this great idea, and I'm always really excited, Paris knows this too, I'm always really hard-pressed. I think we should do this, and they're both like, no. And then I'm like, but why? And then he's like, because you're boring me to death, and then I try to come up with the next one, and then he's like... They don't explain the intellectual reasons why it's really not boring. You're like, try to explain that it's really not boring because people don't know about this and this. Yes, but I sell up falling asleep. No, but you don't understand, it's not boring because it's just new to these people, and they don't know. What you need to do is personally deliver each one of those promotions, and then you can explain it to the prospect. And then at some point I'll say, but, and then I'll lay a phrase on him, and then there'll be this moment of silence, and then he'll say, why didn't you use that? And that becomes our headline. Or, well, when you say it that way, now I'm awake, and then we know that's the headline. But that Paris used to make us do that too. Say it like you're saying it to a friend at a bar, and how are you going to convince me? If you start talking that way, I'm leaving, and I'm taking my drink with me. See, there's no right way. The point is there's no right way to do this. And as much as it varies on this panel, and we've all had tremendous successes, as much as it varies on this panel, for me it varies job to job too. And so, you know, you basically need to be in touch with your muse and see what's working for you, you know, and how the package wants to be written. So, any more questions? Okay. Yeah, a couple quick questions. First one, Paul, you mentioned you had some clients, you know, smaller ones in your hometown. How would you, how'd you find them, and what are the criteria for a good local client? I, well, I just mentioned that as a possibility. I didn't actually use any of that. No, I mean, I know... You need to actually do that for the next day. I know people who have done it, and they simply, there are people who are a lot more extroverted than I am. So it's like, Bigfoot, like this monster, it's a legend, it's supposedly happened to you. I haven't seen it. No, I have first-hand knowledge of this. Okay. No, I know lots of marketing people that I talk with all the time, that's one of their, that's their primary means of getting clients. They just go to different local clients and talk to them about their, yeah, Clayton's one of the ones I've heard it from first. You just go around your local town, there's businesses at every corner that need help marketing themselves, and that's a great place to start because, you know, the expectations, frankly, the expectations are so much lower, you can do just such minor changes to their marketing and that their business will just explode. So, you know, that's a great way to gain confidence, not only in what you're doing and what you're writing and how you're marketing, to learn, but also to gain the confidence, you know, when you start moving up to bigger clients. What do you think has changed with the internet too, because now all these businesses need a webpage and they, like, things they never used to need before? You know, but, you know, in the same way that Clayton had that wonderful list of what to look for in a client, you know, you got to do that with local businesses. Don't just go to the candy store around the corner, go to the guy that has like four plumbing stores or this little franchise, this little chain of yogurt shops that someone has in your area. Because then, you know, it makes sense for them to spend more money. When Joe Polish was doing marketing for carpet cleaners, you guys know who Joe Polish is, right? And he found that people whose company name was Joe's carpet cleaning, not a good prospect, but people that were like Acme carpet cleaning were better prospect. In other words, they considered themselves a business. They were more, you know, likely to partake of stuff like that. And going back to what Paul said, I think the operative words there were great place to start. You do not want to make a career of this. It's just you're not going to make, get enough traction, you're not going to make enough money. It's a great place to get. Like I was saying in my talk, get some things under your belt that you can start showing people to go after the real clients you want. And part of the reason of that too is in the publishing world, you know, you increase, you, a little change and it increases their profit margins so immensely that there's such tremendous leverage that we have. I, you know, I was talking earlier about building up, bringing in a million dollars worth of business for InfoMap when I was getting started. And the way I did it was I would get these Dunn and Bradstreet lists of businesses in different areas. It might be a geographical area or it might be a type of business, a manufacturer under a certain size. And so I had a standard little letter and a little brochure that I would send out to these people and I'd send out a lot of them because I was getting really low responses. But once I got a client closed, I was going to make a lot of money with them so that was okay. So I ended up doing Beverly Hills, I mean, right on Rodeo Drive furniture shop, a bakery that had a special pastry that they could ship through the mail but it never tried before. And a guy who owned a manufacturing company, it was called Chromalite I think, and he had point of use hot water heaters. So, you know, you just turn it on and you have instant hot water no matter where you are. It was pretty cool. They're pretty, you know, they're everywhere now but he was one of the first ones to do this. So, you know, I had the opportunity to do those kinds of campaigns for a lot of different people because I was just going locally there in LA. But again, if you live in Wainsville, North Carolina, you're not going to find very many prospects. But if you're in a big metropolitan area like that, you can, if you spread abroad enough net, you can find some people that really need your services. Second question, is a typical day, how do you divide your time between reading, writing and scouring the news like for financial stuff and trying to keep current and what's your typical day look like as far as... Go ahead. Percentage of each. There's a whole panel on that. You first. It depends on whether I'm working on a health promotion or a financial promotion at the time. I'm trying, Paris taught me a lot of things and one of which was to try to save, if you're a morning person, your morning time should really be your writing time. And then your research time should, and Clayton, you talked about this too, and then your research time should be when you're feeling yourself dragged because it's a little easier on the brain. And I still struggle with this today to get up early and use my morning time only to write and to protect that time. And that's hard, but I try. And then in terms of... So my afternoon is my research because I like, like if I'm working on a boardroom book, I can sit around in the evening and read and highlight things. I can't write bullets. My brain isn't ready for that yet, but I can highlight things that are interesting and make notes and ideas. And then the next morning when I come back to write the bullet, I'm reading it the second and the third time and it's been sifting and sorting through my brain. Finance is a different animal because one of the things that Paras said to me when I first started was, look, you can't always do two fields, but one of the reasons why health and finance go together is because someone doesn't invent a cure for arthritis every morning. Right? So you've got a broad array of information that you have to learn. Am I going to get shot? Does this violate the non-confidentiality agreement? Am I sharing a secret of yours? I think what you're saying is fine. So you have this broad... I'm going to be in touch. You have a broad array. So now you've got to know about arthritis and osteoporosis and heart disease and kidney disease. And you've got to know all the mechanisms of action. But with finance, what you're really doing is trying to stay current. How is this changing all the time? What are people talking about right now? What is the stock market doing? What is the emotional, take the emotional temperature of your investor? So that kind of thing, you might want to start by watching some of the morning news shows or making sure you at least read the left side of the Wall Street Journal, that little short column before you do anything else. When you subscribe to the Wall Street Journal, you get these little alerts for breaking news. I always open and read those to see if something's happened or going on in the market that I need to know about right then. So for me, that may be an exception to a morning rule. I don't chunk my time by day. I chunk it by week. And what I mean by that is one day I'll do nothing but write copy. Another day I'll do nothing but research. Another day I'll do nothing but incidentals, bookkeeping, that sort of thing. And the reason being, it takes most people's brains, it takes my brain, I know far too long to switch gears from one task to another. So I find that I get much more done, I'm much more efficient if I focus one day on a certain task and I just do it that way. That's great. I find that if I spend more than three or four hours writing, I'm fried, I'm done. So I aim to do like three hours of writing. I also think everybody should spend two hours a day reading. And when I say two hours a day reading, I don't mean just reading what the client gave you for the project. I mean just reading about a wide variety of things, magazines, books, news, whatever. You know, you could get an idea for a financial promotion from a knitting magazine. And you could get an idea for an organic gardening promotion from some biker magazine or tattoo magazine. I mean, that's what you want. You want to give your brain lots of food from lots of disparate things because that's what creativity is. What's, as Clain was saying, creativity is connecting two things. So spend two hours a day reading. You just, you got to be voracious readers, you got to be really curious, and you got to just be open to whatever crazy shit's going to come your way. The other thing, I said three hours a day when I'm writing copy, but if I'm writing bullets, I'm fried after like a half hour. So what I do on days when I'm writing bullets is I break it up, and so like maybe you sandwich it between right half hour bullets, go to the gym, come back right another half hour bullets, go eat, come back right another, you know, like that. I just wanted to say something real quick off what he said about the reading. Every Sunday, every Sunday morning, my son and I go to Barnes & Noble, and he goes and he plays in the children's area, and I stand and I read the magazines. And I do nothing but look at the covers and the fascinations, because every one of those magazines has what, millions, billions of dollars of research into them? For just to get fascinations and find out what people are thinking and reading. So that's it. You know, it's funny, I could write bullets all day long. Yeah, and it's what kills me is the narrative, the letter from Marty. That's like torture for me. But bullets, I could just, you know, bullets just, you know, because it's like I know when it's done. You know, I don't want to say too much about how I spend my day, probably because I'm not the best example, but you know, I just want to say one thing, you know, I would like to defy any of you to get up at like five or six every morning to write for three hours with the phone off and the internet off and just spend it writing, and then to spend like an hour or two on new business and the rest of the day reading and studying, and come back and say, I'm not successful. Yeah. I defy you to do that and not be successful. Great. One of the most successful packages I ever had was that health and healing package, the health and healing launch package. And the way that worked was I jumped on an airplane and I flew from Florida to Irvine, California. On the flight, I read the client's book and all of his newspaper articles he'd written. He had a column in the local paper there. So I read that on the way out there. I meet with a client for about an hour hoping to get some passion out of him. You know, why are you doing this? Why are you bucking the medical establishment? And you know, his answer was, I just like to win arguments. Okay, no passion. Okay, I'm out of here. So I go back to my hotel and I had a Tandy laptop, you know, bought it at Radio Shack. The thing was that thick, you know. And I went out by the swimming pool and I sat there and I did my brain dump. And I would write for an hour and I would swim for 15 minutes and then I would write for an hour and then I would swim for 15 minutes. And I just did that all for the rest of the day. By the end of the day, I had a complete Magalogue ready to go. There were a few additional research things that I had to add in. But you know, you listen to Paul McCartney or any of the great songwriters and how do you do it? What's your process? And I'll tell you, first of all, there is no process. Every song comes in its own way. And there are some songs that come already written, it's lyric and even arranged and ready to go. And there are others that come completely different ways. But that idea of working, resting, working, resting really works for me. And then as far as the rest of my process, I gave it to you yesterday. I follow David's approach pretty much. I'm up at five in the morning, whether I want to be or not. My body is just set. Oh, that's fine. Well, let me say David's suggestion will work. Okay, how's that? I'm up every morning at five. I can say whether I want to or not, my eyes open up. So I embrace that. I am definitely an early riser. I'm definitely a morning person. That's my prime time. That's my writing time. So depending on where I am on a project, I try to use the early morning strictly for writing. But I do take breaks, but my breaks are usually things like, to use the other side of the brain, I don't want to pay bills. I want to cook. So I may start making dinner at about 7 a.m. because that's my break. So my family will wake up in the morning going, why do I sound so fake? It's just like, I was just aroma of all kinds of food, but that's a break that I'll take if I get stuck. I write usually until I get stuck somewhere and I'll just stop and I'll do something else. And I'm telling you many times, I'm stuck on something. I need a headline, I need something. And I go upstairs and I do, like I said, I'll cook or make something. And I get the idea and I run down to my office and I leave whatever was on the stove, still on the stove. It burns. We have to go out to dinner that night. Nobody complains, you know, but I have the idea. So that's my process. And I'm working with a client now that's out of the country. So there's a timeframe, big time difference. So I have to take a nap, you know, because my day, I mean, even though my writing time starts 5, 6, 5, 30, 6 o'clock in the morning and goes maybe till about 9, 9, 30. That's like my prime time. But my client doesn't start working until about 2 or 3 in the afternoon, my time. But then I'm usually done working. So I have, I take a nap. I'm big on power naps. I can tell myself, take a nap 15 minutes and wake up and I don't need a clock. I don't need anything. I can do it. And before I take my naps, I tell myself what I need to think, what I have to work on. That totally works. Clayton told me that years ago, I read the book that he talked about. Well, I read the parts he told me to talk about. I didn't read the whole book because it's pretty thick. But it's true. It is, you know, just different things about brain and how it works. So while I'm taking my nap, that's part of my day, my day's work, you know, as far as that's going. Then by 3 o'clock, I'm refreshed and I can go another few, you know, till 6, 7 or so in the evening dealing with work with the other client. And I got a good day in. So it changes if I'm doing a lot of research. I may make sure the research is in the afternoon. But writing, if I'm not writing for a client, I'll write something fun that I like. Maybe I'll write an article that I always wanted to talk, you know, to do and then never got around to it. But write, you know, just get into the, to a rhythm of doing something like that because the juices do flow. They come. I have to agree with Carlino that Gary Halbert had a saying that motion beats meditation. It's very true. So if you're finding yourself stumped, just start writing. I don't know what to write. Blah, blah, blah, blah, blah. It works. And I'm an early riser. So anywhere between 4 and 6 is when I start writing. And usually about 3 hours is about my max, like Paris. After that, I'll go to the gym. And if I still have to write some more, then I can write maybe an hour more. And that's usually about it. The rest of the day is either reading or research and that kind of stuff. My day is probably a little bit different just because of what I do, but I get up around 5 or 6. I have a lot of children that have to get up around 6 o'clock. So if I can grab an hour in the studio before I start carting them around, that's helpful. Then I have this thing every day. So I really am inspired by music. I choose a song of the day. And you guys might know about that already, but a lot of friends in the business will send me a song. A song of the day or a video of the day or something. So that sets the tone of the day depending on what kind of design I need to do. But the thing for me, and I don't know if it is for writers, but for me as a designer, since everything is so sensory, like in visual, I have to get wide open and crack myself wide open. I cannot let someone or something get in there and like taint that openness. I don't know if that's for you. You guys are like, what are you talking about? No, but seriously, each person in this room is going to have to set up their own day and make it work for them. So I will tell you a lot of these odd things I do. So every day I try to leave right at 10.10. I do a whole bunch of design. I go to the gym and I lie like crazy to my clients. They are like, oh yeah, let's do a 10 to 11 appointment. I am like, woo, I am all booked up. Because I am going to the gym. And then I come back and do a little more serious design. And then when you start getting tired near the end of the day, that's when I am like, I will do a meeting. I will do something mindless. But I try to use that big energy for myself in the earlier parts of the day. I have one of those huge yoga balls too. And I will make myself lay backwards and I think whatever it takes. I can tell you again. That works for me. I think for the last hour or so we have been talking a lot about how you have these successful writers. Each one of us has their own approach. And I think that is really important. There is no right way or wrong way. And the key is for you to find out what works for you. And it is okay to write for three hours and just quit. It is okay to write for 30 minutes and just quit. If that works for you. And it obviously works really well for Paris. So, of course you have got to remember that Paris has copy writers doing all his writing for him now. So he is a master in finding other people to do his work. I am sure you are, baby. Yeah. I just want to thank you guys for being here and for being a part of this. It has meant the world to me personally and also to our experts. The primary message of this weekend though really, get back to it. We have given you a lot of tools for building your business. The key now is to pick the tools that will work best for you. And you are given your personality, your skills and your niche. And to create that plan that we talked about. These are the things I am going to do. These are the days I am going to do them on. And so after 30 days or 60 days, whatever your process is, you have got your well on your way to generating good business, good clients. Making good deals, getting good contracts. It is a process. It is something you have to do methodically. If you do not, who was it talking about procrastination today? Was it G? Yeah. And it cracked me up because that is exactly what we do. That is what I do with mostly doctor's appointments, you know, stuff like that. Dentist appointments. But with copywriting, I learned a long time ago that procrastination is just stealing time. You can't get the time back. No, you can't get it back. And that applies not just to copywriting. It applies to building your business. Realize that what you are doing in building your business is it is serious work. I mean, it is as serious as if you were working for a GORA and they had a corporate mission of some kind or a package that needed to be written or something else. You know, value yourself enough to know that in your world, in your universe, you are more important than anything else that is going on. And what you do for yourself in terms of building that business is going to pay dividends to you, you know, forever. And it also gives you, remember we were talking about that whoever it was, it stuck that word evil into the Bible where it said, Bunny is the root of all evil. You know, it really is Bunny is the root of all. You know, and you got to think that way. You got to focus on that. You got to realize that it gives you the ability to help people, to make a good life for your family, to make a good life for yourself. We are all only here for a very short period of time. Our most limited resource is time, you know, and so, and remember time is money. So you've got to get the most for every hour you spend working. The fruits of this conference are really going to be felt by everybody. And I really look forward to seeing you all on the way out. Thank you.
